results.possibleIdentity: John Allen Strange (1943–2008), widely known as Allen Strange, was an influential American composer, performer, and author who specialized in electronic music and avant-garde performance. Active from the late 1960s through the early 21st century, he was a key figure in the intersection of art and technology. Strange served as a Professor of Music and director of the electronic music studios at San Jose State University starting in 1970. He is perhaps best known for his 1972 book, 'Electronic Music: Systems, Techniques, and Controls,' which became a definitive pedagogical text for analog synthesis. His career was marked by pioneering live electronic performance; he co-founded the ensemble BIOME (1967–1972) to explore the EMS Synthi synthesizer and later co-founded the Electric Weasel Ensemble in 1974 with synthesizer designer Donald Buchla. He was also an active member of the Leonardo/International Society for the Arts, Sciences, and Technology (ISAST) advisory committee, an organization that, like Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), promoted the convergence of these fields. His work often involved interdisciplinary collaborations, including compositions for the theater and live electronics with acoustic instruments.

results.possibleIdentity: James Lennon is a Californian architect and researcher who has been active in the fields of environmental psychology and design since at least the early 1970s. He is the principal of Lennon Associates, based in Del Mar, California, and is a member of the American Institute of Architects (AIA). Lennon is notably recognized for an experimental project in which he developed large, vertically hung transparent panels featuring abstracted "frowning eyes." These panels were installed in retail stores as a psychological experiment to deter shoplifting, leveraging the concept of "the power of the look" (or the "evil eye") to influence human behavior. This work reflects the interdisciplinary approach championed by the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization, which sought to bridge the gaps between artists, architects, and engineers through the application of technology and psychology to the built environment. In his later career, he specialized in the design and optimization of healthcare facilities, specifically Emergency Departments, where he pioneered "split-flow" and "chair-centric" models to improve patient care and operational efficiency.

James Lennon (1953–2009) was an artist based in Coalinga and Fresno, California, known for his unique style of drawing and painting. He was active in the local art scene starting in the late 1960s and early 1970s, having graduated from Coalinga High School in 1971. Described as an avid lover of history and art, he spent decades developing a personal artistic practice that was recognized for its gentle nature and distinct visual humor.
